stem: You are the medical registrar reviewing a 56-year-old woman with new-onset painful rash for 36 hours after starting allopurinol 100 mg daily 3 weeks ago for gout. She now has flaccid bullae over the trunk that detach with gentle pressure, oral and conjunctival erosions, and a fever. The dermatology consultant on call estimates 18 percent body surface area involvement with epidermal detachment.

data_table.Examination: Flaccid bullae trunk and arms; Nikolsky positive; oral erosions; conjunctival injection with erosions; dusky targetoid lesions
data_table.Investigations: Hb 132, WCC 6.8, Cr 142 (baseline 76), ALT 122, ALP 184, albumin 28, CRP 96; SCORTEN calculated as 3
data_table.Medications: Allopurinol 100 mg daily for 3 weeks; nil other recent

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Cease allopurinol and transfer to a burns or specialised dermatology unit for supportive care and wound management
options.rationale: Stevens-Johnson syndrome and toxic epidermal necrolysis are managed by immediate cessation of the culprit drug and transfer to a burns or specialised dermatology unit; supportive care with fluid balance, wound care, infection control and ocular care is the cornerstone. (Source: Australasian Society of Clinical Immunology and Allergy: SCAR)
source_refs: Australasian Society of Clinical Immunology and Allergy: SCAR

options.text: Continue allopurinol; commence oral prednisolone 50 mg daily and topical corticosteroid; review at 48 hours by dermatology
options.rationale: Continuing the culprit drug is dangerous; systemic steroids in SJS/TEN have unclear benefit; cessation and supportive care are the priority. (Source: ASCIA: SCAR)
source_refs: Australasian Society of Clinical Immunology and Allergy: SCAR

options.text: Cease allopurinol; commence ciclosporin 3 mg/kg twice daily orally with bedside isolation in the medical ward
options.rationale: Ciclosporin has emerging evidence in TEN but is not first-line; specialised burns or dermatology unit transfer takes priority over ward-level care. (Source: ASCIA: SCAR)
source_refs: Australasian Society of Clinical Immunology and Allergy: SCAR

options.text: Cease allopurinol; commence IV intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg over 4 days in current medical ward setting
options.rationale: IVIG is controversial in SJS/TEN with conflicting evidence; transfer to specialised unit is the priority intervention. (Source: ASCIA: SCAR)
source_refs: Australasian Society of Clinical Immunology and Allergy: SCAR

options.text: Continue allopurinol; commence broad-spectrum IV antibiotics empirically and arrange skin biopsy in 48 hours for confirmation
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics in SJS/TEN without infection signs increase nosocomial resistance; biopsy can be done but should not delay cessation and transfer. (Source: ASCIA: SCAR)
source_refs: Australasian Society of Clinical Immunology and Allergy: SCAR

explanation.summary: Stevens-Johnson syndrome (less than 10 percent BSA detachment), SJS/TEN overlap (10 to 30 percent) and TEN (greater than 30 percent) are dermatological emergencies. Immediate management is cessation of the culprit drug and transfer to a burns or specialised dermatology unit. Supportive care includes fluid balance, wound care, ocular surface protection, nutrition and infection surveillance. The role of systemic therapies (ciclosporin, IVIG, etanercept, steroids) is debated; SCORTEN at 24 to 72 hours predicts mortality.
key_points: SJS/TEN: cease culprit drug immediately and transfer to burns/dermatology unit
Supportive care is the foundation; specific immunomodulatory therapy is debated
Allopurinol, lamotrigine, sulfonamides, anti-epileptics are common triggers
HLA-B*5801 testing reduces allopurinol risk in high-risk populations
explanation.pearls: SCORTEN parameters: age over 40, malignancy, HR over 120, BSA over 10 percent, urea over 10, glucose over 14, bicarbonate under 20; each adds 1 to predicted mortality.

sources.label: Australasian Society of Clinical Immunology and Allergy: SCAR

stem: You are the medical registrar reviewing a 58-year-old man in clinic 6 months after a first unprovoked submassive pulmonary embolism. He has been on apixaban 5 mg twice daily and is asymptomatic with normal exercise tolerance. He has no malignancy on age-appropriate screening and no thrombophilia screen abnormalities. He has no high bleeding risk and is keen to continue if benefit outweighs harm.

data_table.Examination: Well; no oedema; chest clear; no calf swelling
data_table.Investigations: Hb 142, platelets 240, INR not applicable; eGFR 84; LFTs normal; thrombophilia screen negative; CT chest 6 weeks ago shows resolution of PE; HAS-BLED 1

lead_in: What is the most appropriate anticoagulation strategy beyond 6 months?

options.text: Cease apixaban; arrange repeat D-dimer at 4 weeks off therapy and consider reinitiation only if D-dimer is positive
options.rationale: Off-treatment D-dimer informs risk but is not sufficient alone for cessation in unprovoked PE; extended anticoagulation is generally preferred when bleeding risk is low. (Source: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand: VTE Guidelines)

options.text: Continue apixaban at reduced dose 2.5 mg twice daily indefinitely; review annually for bleeding risk and goals of care
options.rationale: After 6 months for unprovoked PE with low bleeding risk, extended therapy with reduced-dose apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily reduces recurrent VTE without significantly increasing major bleeding (AMPLIFY-EXT data); annual review balances ongoing benefit. (Source: THANZ VTE Guidelines)

options.text: Switch to warfarin INR 2 to 3 indefinitely; arrange community INR monitoring weekly initially then monthly when stable
options.rationale: DOACs are preferred over warfarin for VTE in absence of antiphospholipid syndrome or other contraindication; warfarin offers no advantage and adds monitoring burden. (Source: THANZ VTE Guidelines)

options.text: Switch to aspirin 100 mg daily; arrange GP review in 6 months and resume DOAC if recurrent VTE symptoms develop
options.rationale: Aspirin reduces recurrent VTE by only about 30 percent compared with around 80 percent for low-dose DOAC; not preferred when DOAC is tolerated. (Source: THANZ VTE Guidelines)

options.text: Cease apixaban at 6 months; advise that the risk of recurrent VTE is sufficiently low after 6 months of therapy to stop
options.rationale: Recurrent VTE risk after unprovoked event is 10 percent at 1 year and 30 percent at 5 years after stopping anticoagulation; extended therapy is generally indicated when bleeding risk is acceptable. (Source: THANZ VTE Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Following a first unprovoked PE, anticoagulation should generally continue beyond 3 to 6 months if bleeding risk is acceptable. The AMPLIFY-EXT and EINSTEIN-CHOICE trials demonstrated that reduced-dose apixaban (2.5 mg twice daily) or rivaroxaban (10 mg daily) effectively reduce recurrent VTE without significantly increasing major bleeding compared with full-dose. Bleeding risk should be reviewed annually. Provoked VTE with a transient major risk factor typically requires only 3 months of anticoagulation.
key_points: Unprovoked PE: extended anticoagulation beyond 6 months if bleeding risk acceptable
Reduced-dose apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily preferred for extended phase
Annual review of bleeding risk and goals of care
Aspirin alone is much less effective than reduced-dose DOAC
explanation.pearls: Provoked VTE with transient major risk factor (surgery, trauma, immobility) typically warrants only 3 months of anticoagulation; cancer-associated VTE requires DOAC or LMWH for 6 months minimum.

stem: You are the medical registrar reviewing a 52-year-old man with 3 weeks of fever, fatigue and weight loss. He had a dental extraction 6 weeks ago. He has a history of bicuspid aortic valve. Examination reveals a new diastolic murmur, splinter haemorrhages and a tender nodule on his left index finger pulp. Echocardiography shows aortic valve vegetation 8 mm and moderate aortic regurgitation. Two of three sets of blood cultures grow viridans group streptococci.

data_table.Examination: New 2/6 diastolic decrescendo murmur LSE; splinter haemorrhages; Osler node left index finger pulp; no Janeway lesion; spleen tip palpable
data_table.Investigations: WCC 13.4, Hb 108, CRP 96, ESR 78, urine dipstick haematuria +, TTE aortic vegetation 8 mm, moderate AR; blood cultures 2 of 3 sets viridans streptococci

lead_in: Which best characterises the diagnostic certainty under the modified Duke criteria?

options.text: Possible endocarditis (1 major plus 1 minor criterion); commence empirical IV flucloxacillin pending further cultures
options.rationale: This patient meets definite criteria with 2 major (typical organism with 2 sets of blood cultures positive plus vegetation on echo) and multiple minor criteria; treatment is targeted not empirical. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Possible endocarditis (2 minor criteria only); arrange TOE within 24 hours before commencing any antimicrobial therapy
options.rationale: The criteria fulfilled exceed possible; TOE may further define vegetation but should not delay treatment. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Definite endocarditis (2 major criteria); commence IV benzylpenicillin 1.8 g 4-hourly for 4 weeks with gentamicin for the first 2 weeks
options.rationale: Two major Duke criteria (typical organism in 2 cultures plus vegetation with regurgitation) confirm definite endocarditis; targeted treatment for viridans strep native valve endocarditis is benzylpenicillin 4 weeks with gentamicin for the first 2 weeks. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Rejected endocarditis (alternate diagnosis); arrange dental review and broad febrile workup including bone scan and PET
options.rationale: Rejected requires firm alternate diagnosis or resolution within 4 days of antibiotic; this patient has classic endocarditis features. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Definite endocarditis (3 minor criteria); commence IV vancomycin and ciprofloxacin pending speciation and sensitivities
options.rationale: Vancomycin plus quinolone is incorrect empirical cover for native-valve streptococcal endocarditis; species is already identified as viridans group streptococci. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: The modified Duke criteria diagnose infective endocarditis as definite, possible or rejected. Major criteria include typical organism in 2 separate blood cultures and evidence of endocardial involvement on echocardiography (vegetation, abscess, new valve regurgitation). Minor criteria include predisposing valve disease, fever above 38, vascular phenomena (Janeway, emboli) and immunological phenomena (Osler nodes, Roth spots, glomerulonephritis). Two majors, or one major plus 3 minors, or 5 minors satisfy definite endocarditis. Targeted antimicrobial therapy is selected by organism and valve type.
key_points: Two majors satisfy definite endocarditis
Native valve viridans strep: benzylpenicillin 4 weeks plus gentamicin 2 weeks
Surgical indications: heart failure, abscess, large mobile vegetation, persistent bacteraemia
TOE indicated if TTE non-diagnostic or for prosthetic valves
explanation.pearls: Surgery within 7 days is indicated for heart failure from valve dysfunction, perivalvular abscess, or persistent bacteraemia despite 5 to 7 days of appropriate antibiotic.

stem: You are the medical registrar called to a 64-year-old man with severe COPD on the respiratory ward who has acutely deteriorated. He developed sudden right-sided chest pain and severe dyspnoea 5 minutes ago. He is hypotensive, tachycardic and using accessory muscles. There is decreased breath sounds and hyperresonance on the right with tracheal deviation to the left. SpO2 is 84 percent on 15 L non-rebreather.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 78/44, HR 132, RR 36, SpO2 84% on 15 L NRB, T 36.6, GCS 13
data_table.Examination: Severe respiratory distress, accessory muscle use; trachea deviated to the left; right chest hyperresonant with absent breath sounds; distended neck veins
data_table.Investigations: Cannot wait for imaging; ECG sinus tachycardia

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate action?

options.text: Arrange urgent portable chest X-ray to confirm tension pneumothorax; commence non-invasive ventilation while awaiting result
options.rationale: Tension pneumothorax is a clinical diagnosis; awaiting imaging or starting NIV worsens the tension and delays life-saving decompression. (Source: ANZCOR Guidelines)

options.text: Administer IV crystalloid 1 L bolus; commence noradrenaline infusion to support BP while preparing for theatre
options.rationale: Volume and vasopressor without decompression do not address the obstructive shock; decompression is the only effective treatment. (Source: ATLS Australia)

options.text: Intubate immediately with rocuronium and ketamine; apply positive pressure ventilation to recruit collapsed lung tissue
options.rationale: Positive pressure ventilation in tension pneumothorax worsens the tension and can precipitate cardiac arrest; decompression is required before intubation. (Source: ATLS Australia)

options.text: Perform finger thoracostomy at the 5th intercostal space mid-axillary line on the right; insert intercostal catheter immediately after
options.rationale: Immediate finger thoracostomy in the safe triangle (5th ICS mid-axillary) followed by ICC is the current preferred technique for tension pneumothorax due to reliability in adults with chest wall thickness; needle decompression at 2nd ICS mid-clavicular is an alternative with higher failure rate. (Source: ATLS Australia)

options.text: Administer salbutamol 5 mg and ipratropium 500 micrograms nebulised; increase prednisolone to 50 mg orally daily
options.rationale: Nebulisers and steroids treat COPD exacerbation not tension pneumothorax; do not address the obstructive physiology. (Source: ANZCOR Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Tension pneumothorax is a clinical diagnosis based on respiratory distress, hypotension, ipsilateral hyperresonance, absent breath sounds, tracheal deviation and distended neck veins. Immediate decompression is required without waiting for imaging. Australian and ATLS guidance increasingly favours finger thoracostomy at the safe triangle (5th intercostal space anterior to mid-axillary line) over the traditional 2nd intercostal space mid-clavicular needle decompression because of high failure rates with needles in adults. Definitive intercostal catheter follows immediately. Positive pressure ventilation before decompression is dangerous.
key_points: Tension pneumothorax is a clinical diagnosis; treat before imaging
Finger thoracostomy at 5th ICS mid-axillary is preferred over needle at 2nd ICS
Avoid positive pressure ventilation before decompression
ICC follows immediately after decompression
explanation.pearls: If using needle decompression as first step, a longer 14G cannula (at least 8 cm) is recommended because the standard short cannula fails to reach the pleural space in over half of adults.

stem: You are the medical registrar reviewing a 46-year-old woman with resistant hypertension on perindopril 10 mg, amlodipine 10 mg and hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily. Despite triple therapy her BP remains 162/96. She has muscle cramps and her potassium has consistently been at the lower end (3.0 to 3.4). She has no symptoms of phaeochromocytoma. There is no family history of hypertension before age 40.

data_table.Examination: Well; no abdominal bruit; no Cushingoid features; no goitre; no neurological deficit
data_table.Investigations: Na 142, K 3.2, Cr 78, eGFR 78, plasma renin activity low, aldosterone elevated, ARR pending; TSH normal; HbA1c 5.6%; ECG sinus rhythm; urine ACR 2.4
data_table.Medications: Perindopril 10 mg daily; amlodipine 10 mg daily; hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: 1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test; continue current antihypertensive regimen without change before testing
options.rationale: DST screens for Cushing's, not aldosteronism; ARR is the appropriate first test for this clinical picture. (Source: Endocrine Society of Australia: Primary Aldosteronism)
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia: Primary Aldosteronism

options.text: 24-hour urinary fractionated metanephrines; correct potassium and continue all current antihypertensives during collection
options.rationale: Metanephrines test for phaeochromocytoma; clinical picture is aldosteronism with hypokalaemia; ARR is the appropriate screen. (Source: Endocrine Society of Australia: Primary Aldosteronism)
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia: Primary Aldosteronism

options.text: Renal artery duplex ultrasound; commence empirical spironolactone 25 mg daily while awaiting the result of imaging
options.rationale: Renovascular disease is a differential but ARR screen for aldosteronism comes first given hypokalaemia; spironolactone confounds ARR if started before sampling. (Source: Endocrine Society of Australia: Primary Aldosteronism)
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia: Primary Aldosteronism

options.text: Late-night salivary cortisol; reduce hydrochlorothiazide to 12.5 mg daily and continue ACE inhibitor at full dose
options.rationale: Late-night salivary cortisol screens for Cushing's; no Cushingoid features present and hypokalaemia with resistant hypertension is more characteristic of aldosteronism. (Source: Endocrine Society of Australia: Primary Aldosteronism)
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia: Primary Aldosteronism

options.text: Aldosterone-to-renin ratio screening; correct potassium and switch ACE inhibitor to verapamil before sampling for accuracy
options.rationale: ARR is the screening test for primary hyperaldosteronism in resistant hypertension; medications that affect the RAAS (ACEi, ARB, beta blocker, diuretic) should be stopped where possible and replaced with non-interfering agents like verapamil or doxazosin before sampling, and hypokalaemia corrected. (Source: Endocrine Society of Australia: Primary Aldosteronism)
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia: Primary Aldosteronism

explanation.summary: Primary aldosteronism is the commonest secondary cause of hypertension and is often missed because hypokalaemia is present in only about a third of cases. Screening with the aldosterone-to-renin ratio is indicated for resistant hypertension, hypertension with hypokalaemia, hypertension with adrenal incidentaloma, hypertension under 40 and hypertension with first-degree family history of aldosteronism or early stroke. Hypokalaemia should be corrected and interfering medications swapped before testing. Confirmatory testing (saline suppression or fludrocortisone suppression) follows a positive ARR; adrenal vein sampling guides surgical decision-making.
key_points: ARR is the screening test for primary aldosteronism
Correct hypokalaemia and use non-interfering antihypertensives before sampling
Confirmatory testing follows positive ARR
Adrenal vein sampling guides surgical decisions
explanation.pearls: Verapamil and doxazosin do not interfere significantly with ARR and are the preferred bridging antihypertensives during workup.

sources.label: Endocrine Society of Australia: Primary Aldosteronism

stem: You are the obstetrics registrar reviewing a 28-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes on a basal-bolus insulin regimen. She is planning her first pregnancy in the next 6 months. Her last HbA1c is 8.4 percent. She has background diabetic retinopathy and microalbuminuria. She is on perindopril 5 mg daily and atorvastatin 20 mg nocte. Her partner is well.

data_table.Examination: Well; BMI 26; no peripheral oedema; fundoscopy background retinopathy
data_table.Investigations: HbA1c 8.4%, eGFR 84, urine ACR 5.2, TSH 1.4, fT4 normal, beta-hCG negative
data_table.Medications: Insulin glargine 24 units nocte; insulin aspart 6 to 10 units with meals; perindopril 5 mg daily; atorvastatin 20 mg nocte

lead_in: What is the most important preconception management package?

options.text: Cease perindopril and atorvastatin before conception; commence folic acid 5 mg daily; target HbA1c below 6.5 percent if achievable safely
options.rationale: ACE inhibitors and statins are teratogenic and ceased before conception; folic acid 5 mg daily reduces neural tube defects in high-risk women; tight glycaemic control reduces malformation and stillbirth risk. (Source: ADIPS Preconception Care Guidelines)

options.text: Continue current medications; commence folic acid 500 micrograms daily; advise conception once HbA1c is 7 percent
options.rationale: Perindopril and atorvastatin are teratogenic and must be ceased; folic acid 5 mg daily is required for high-risk women; HbA1c target is below 6.5 percent if achievable safely. (Source: ADIPS Preconception Care Guidelines)

options.text: Continue perindopril and atorvastatin until conception; commence folic acid 500 micrograms daily; target HbA1c below 8 percent
options.rationale: Continuing ACE inhibitor and statin to conception risks fetal exposure during organogenesis; both should be ceased preconception. (Source: ADIPS Preconception Care Guidelines)

options.text: Switch insulin to oral metformin and gliclazide before pregnancy; commence folic acid 5 mg daily; target HbA1c below 7 percent
options.rationale: Type 1 diabetes requires insulin; gliclazide is not used in pregnancy; metformin is added in selected cases of T2DM, not T1DM. (Source: ADIPS Preconception Care Guidelines)

options.text: Defer pregnancy planning until microalbuminuria resolves; continue all current medications and review HbA1c quarterly only
options.rationale: Microalbuminuria does not preclude pregnancy but warrants nephrology input; deferring without active counselling is not appropriate. (Source: ADIPS Preconception Care Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Preconception care in pregestational diabetes is central to reducing congenital malformations, miscarriage and stillbirth. Key elements include high-dose folic acid 5 mg daily for at least 3 months preconception, optimisation of HbA1c below 6.5 percent (or as close as safely achievable), cessation of teratogenic medications (ACE inhibitors, ARBs, statins, certain antihypertensives), screening for diabetic complications (retinopathy, nephropathy), and contraception until ready. Multidisciplinary care involving diabetes-in-pregnancy specialists is recommended.
key_points: Folic acid 5 mg daily for high-risk women including diabetes
HbA1c target below 6.5 percent before conception if safely achievable
Cease ACE inhibitors, ARBs and statins before conception
Screen for retinopathy and nephropathy before pregnancy
explanation.pearls: Risk of major congenital malformation rises with HbA1c: approximately 2 percent at HbA1c 6 percent, 5 percent at 8 percent, 12 percent at 10 percent.

subtopic_detail: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy recognition
stem: You are the obstetric registrar called to ED to review a 32-year-old G2P1 at 36+4 weeks with 5 days of nausea, vomiting, malaise and now confusion. Her partner reports she is sleepy and not making sense. She has had no antenatal complications. She has no headache, no visual disturbance, no abdominal pain. Her BP is 132/82, urine dipstick shows trace protein only. Her bloods are striking.

data_table.Examination: Jaundiced sclerae, mild RUQ tenderness, no peripheral oedema, fetal heart 146 with variable decelerations on CTG
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 2.4, ammonia 96, ALT 380, AST 420, ALP 280, bilirubin 78, INR 2.4, fibrinogen 1.4, platelets 168, Cr 124 (baseline 64), urate 0.46, lactate 4.6, urine dipstick protein trace; haptoglobin normal; LDH 480

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Administer betamethasone 11.4 mg IM and observe for 24 hours for spontaneous improvement; defer delivery for steroid effect
options.rationale: AFLP is a maternal emergency requiring urgent delivery regardless of gestation beyond 34 weeks; awaiting steroid effect or observing risks rapid maternal decompensation. (Source: RANZCOG Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy

options.text: Correct hypoglycaemia and coagulopathy; arrange immediate delivery typically by caesarean; transfer to a centre with HDU and adult ICU
options.rationale: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy requires urgent delivery, correction of hypoglycaemia and coagulopathy, and tertiary multidisciplinary care; survival depends on prompt delivery and supportive care. (Source: RANZCOG Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy

options.text: Administer magnesium sulfate 4 g IV bolus and arrange induction of labour with vaginal delivery aim within 24 to 48 hours
options.rationale: Magnesium is for eclampsia prophylaxis in pre-eclampsia, not AFLP; vaginal delivery is acceptable in stable AFLP but delivery should be expedited not deferred; coagulopathy may favour caesarean. (Source: RANZCOG Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy

options.text: Administer N-acetylcysteine 150 mg/kg IV loading dose; observe for 48 hours and consider delivery if liver function does not improve
options.rationale: NAC is used in paracetamol-induced liver failure with debated benefit in AFLP; observation alone delays delivery and risks fetal and maternal death. (Source: RANZCOG Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy

options.text: Arrange urgent liver biopsy to differentiate AFLP from HELLP syndrome before deciding on delivery timing and mode
options.rationale: Liver biopsy in coagulopathic peripartum patients is dangerous and unnecessary; AFLP is a clinical and biochemical diagnosis with Swansea criteria; delivery should not wait for histology. (Source: RANZCOG Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy

explanation.summary: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy is a rare but life-threatening late-third-trimester emergency, characterised by maternal hypoglycaemia, coagulopathy with low fibrinogen, hyperammonaemia, mildly elevated transaminases and acute kidney injury. The Swansea criteria support diagnosis. Distinguishing features from HELLP include hypoglycaemia, coagulopathy out of proportion to platelet count, and absence of marked haemolysis. Treatment is urgent delivery, correction of hypoglycaemia and coagulopathy, and tertiary supportive care. Many patients improve rapidly after delivery; some require ICU and rarely liver transplantation.
key_points: Hypoglycaemia, coagulopathy and AKI distinguish AFLP from HELLP
Urgent delivery is the only definitive treatment
Caesarean is often preferred due to coagulopathy and CTG abnormalities
Tertiary multidisciplinary care including HDU and ICU
explanation.pearls: LCHAD deficiency in the fetus is associated with AFLP; postpartum genetic screening of the neonate is offered.

sources.label: RANZCOG Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy

stem: You are the gynaecology registrar reviewing a 24-year-old woman in ED with 6 hours of sudden severe right iliac fossa pain, nausea and one vomit. Her LMP was 14 days ago and beta-hCG is negative. She is not on contraception and has no STI risk. Her transvaginal ultrasound shows a 6 cm right ovarian dermoid cyst, an enlarged oedematous right ovary, and absent venous Doppler flow on the right with maintained arterial flow.

data_table.Examination: Tender right iliac fossa with guarding; cervix closed; no PV bleeding; no cervical excitation
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG negative; WCC 12.4; CRP 22; transvaginal US right ovary 6 cm dermoid, enlarged ovary, absent venous Doppler flow, arterial flow preserved; left ovary normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Conservative management with IV opiate analgesia; arrange outpatient gynaecology follow-up to monitor cyst progression by ultrasound
options.rationale: Conservative management of confirmed torsion leads to ovarian necrosis; surgery is required regardless of symptom severity. (Source: RANZCOG Ovarian Torsion Statement)

options.text: MRI pelvis to confirm diagnosis and degree of vascular compromise before booking elective laparoscopy within 24 to 48 hours
options.rationale: Imaging delay risks ovarian loss; the diagnosis is established on US with characteristic findings and clinical picture; surgery is required urgently. (Source: RANZCOG Ovarian Torsion Statement)

options.text: Urgent laparoscopy with detorsion and ovarian conservation; cystectomy of the dermoid cyst at the same procedure if feasible
options.rationale: Ovarian torsion is a surgical emergency; laparoscopic detorsion with ovarian conservation and cystectomy is the standard procedure even when the ovary appears compromised, because ovarian function commonly recovers. (Source: RANZCOG Ovarian Torsion Statement)

options.text: Urgent laparotomy with right salpingo-oophorectomy due to ovarian non-viability and the presence of a complex cystic mass
options.rationale: Oophorectomy is reserved for postmenopausal women or clearly necrotic ovary; black or congested appearance after detorsion is not an indication for removal in reproductive-age women. (Source: RANZCOG Ovarian Torsion Statement)

options.text: Diagnostic laparoscopy first; if torsion is confirmed convert to laparotomy and proceed with right oophorectomy and biopsy
options.rationale: Laparoscopy is the definitive surgical approach and can include detorsion and cystectomy in the same procedure; routine conversion to laparotomy and oophorectomy is not appropriate. (Source: RANZCOG Ovarian Torsion Statement)

explanation.summary: Ovarian torsion is a surgical emergency. Ultrasound features include an enlarged oedematous ovary, ovarian mass or cyst, and abnormal or absent Doppler flow (venous flow is lost before arterial). Diagnosis is supported by clinical picture and imaging but normal Doppler does not exclude torsion. Treatment is urgent laparoscopic detorsion with ovarian conservation, even if the ovary appears dusky or congested. Cystectomy may be performed in the same procedure if a clear lead point is present. Oophorectomy is reserved for postmenopausal women or clearly necrotic ovary.
key_points: Torsion is a clinical and ultrasound diagnosis; treat urgently
Detorsion with ovarian conservation is preferred even if dusky
Venous flow lost before arterial; normal Doppler does not exclude torsion
Oophorectomy reserved for postmenopausal or necrotic ovary
explanation.pearls: Recurrent torsion can occur; oophoropexy may be considered after a second event in the same ovary or when no anatomical lead point is identified.

stem: You are the GP registrar reviewing a 22-year-old woman who attends today for emergency contraception. She had unprotected vaginal intercourse 96 hours ago. Her LMP was 14 days ago in a regular 28-day cycle. She is on no regular medications, has BMI 32 and is not breastfeeding. She has no contraindications to copper IUD. She is keen on the most effective option available.

data_table.Examination: Well; no abdominal tenderness; cervix appears normal on speculum exam
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG negative today; STI screen pending

lead_in: Which is the most effective emergency contraceptive option for this patient?

options.text: Levonorgestrel 1.5 mg oral once now and advise abstinence or barrier method until next menstrual period commences
options.rationale: Levonorgestrel is licensed for use up to 72 hours and is less effective in higher BMI; copper IUD is more effective and remains effective at this timepoint. (Source: RANZCOG Emergency Contraception)

options.text: Levonorgestrel 3 mg oral once now (double dose for BMI above 26) plus combined oral contraceptive quick start tomorrow
options.rationale: Double-dose levonorgestrel is sometimes used in BMI above 26 but evidence is limited and it remains less effective than copper IUD at 96 hours. (Source: RANZCOG Emergency Contraception)

options.text: Ulipristal acetate 30 mg oral once now and advise no progestogen-containing contraception starts for 5 days afterwards
options.rationale: Ulipristal is licensed up to 120 hours but is also less effective than copper IUD; copper IUD is preferred when patient is suitable. (Source: RANZCOG Emergency Contraception)

options.text: Insertion of a copper intrauterine device within 24 to 48 hours by trained inserter; also screen and treat for chlamydia presumptively
options.rationale: Copper IUD is the most effective emergency contraception (pregnancy rate below 0.1 percent) and works up to 5 days after unprotected intercourse or up to 5 days after expected ovulation; suitable in higher BMI. (Source: RANZCOG Emergency Contraception)

options.text: Misoprostol 800 micrograms vaginally once now and arrange beta-hCG quantitative testing in 14 days for confirmation of contraception
options.rationale: Misoprostol is used for medical termination or miscarriage management, not for emergency contraception. (Source: RANZCOG Emergency Contraception)

explanation.summary: Emergency contraception options in Australia include levonorgestrel oral up to 72 hours, ulipristal acetate oral up to 120 hours, and copper intrauterine device up to 5 days after unprotected intercourse or up to 5 days after the earliest expected ovulation. Copper IUD is the most effective with pregnancy rate below 0.1 percent and is preferred when accessible, especially in higher BMI where oral options are less effective. Ulipristal is more effective than levonorgestrel in the 72 to 120 hour window and around the time of ovulation but is contraindicated in breastfeeding for 7 days after dose. STI screening is offered at insertion.
key_points: Copper IUD is the most effective emergency contraceptive (less than 0.1 percent pregnancy)
Window: 5 days post-intercourse or 5 days post-ovulation
Ulipristal up to 120 hours; levonorgestrel up to 72 hours
Oral methods less effective in higher BMI
explanation.pearls: Ulipristal can reduce efficacy of subsequent progestogen contraception; delay start of hormonal contraception for 5 days after ulipristal dose.

subtopic_detail: Refractory atonic PPH after first-line uterotonics
stem:
You are the obstetrics registrar called to the birthing suite for a 32-year-old G3P3 at 5 minutes post normal vaginal delivery of a 4200 g infant after a prolonged second stage. She has had a brisk PV bleed of approximately 1200 mL. The uterus is boggy and not contracting despite fundal massage. She has received oxytocin 10 IU IM with delivery of placenta.

The placenta appears complete. Her BP is 92/56 and HR 122. The team has called for help, set up large-bore IV access, and the massive transfusion protocol has been activated.

data_table.Examination: Uterus boggy at umbilicus; ongoing PV blood loss; no cervical or vaginal laceration on inspection; placenta examined and complete
data_table.Investigations: Hb 88 (was 124 antenatal), platelets 184, fibrinogen 2.4, INR 1.1; group and hold sent; massive transfusion protocol activated; blood gas pH 7.32 lactate 2.4
data_table.Medications: Oxytocin 10 IU IM with delivery; no current antihypertensive history; no contraindication to ergometrine

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.text: Carboprost 250 micrograms intramyometrial; repeat every 15 minutes to a maximum of 8 doses; avoid in asthma or pulmonary hypertension
options.rationale: Carboprost is appropriate later in the algorithm but ergometrine and oxytocin infusion come first when not contraindicated; carboprost is third line in Australian practice. (Source: RANZCOG PPH Management)

options.text: Calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL IV over 10 minutes; defer further uterotonics until ionised calcium is rechecked at 30 minutes
options.rationale: Calcium supplementation may be needed during massive transfusion but is not the next uterotonic step; ongoing PPH requires escalating uterotonic. (Source: RANZCOG PPH Management)

options.text: Misoprostol 800 micrograms rectal; observe over 30 minutes for response; avoid further uterotonic during this interval
options.rationale: Misoprostol has a slower onset than ergometrine and is generally used when injectable uterotonics are unavailable or contraindicated; not first escalation. (Source: RANZCOG PPH Management)

options.text: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV over 10 minutes alone and observe for 30 minutes before further uterotonics; recheck fibrinogen at 60 minutes
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV is given as adjunctive therapy ideally within 3 hours but does not replace escalating uterotonic; observation alone is dangerous in active atonic PPH. (Source: RANZCOG PPH Management)

options.text: Ergometrine 250 micrograms IM and start oxytocin infusion 40 IU in 1 L crystalloid over 4 hours; reassess at 5 minutes for tone
options.rationale: Second-line uterotonic after oxytocin bolus failure is ergometrine 250 micrograms IM with simultaneous oxytocin infusion; ergometrine is avoided in hypertension or pre-eclampsia but is appropriate here. (Source: RANZCOG PPH Management)

explanation.summary: Postpartum haemorrhage management follows the four T mnemonic (tone, trauma, tissue, thrombin). Atonic PPH refractory to oxytocin bolus is escalated with ergometrine 250 micrograms IM (avoided in hypertension and pre-eclampsia) and an oxytocin infusion of 40 IU in 1 L crystalloid over 4 hours. Carboprost 250 micrograms IM or intramyometrial is third line and avoided in asthma or pulmonary hypertension. Misoprostol is an alternative. Tranexamic acid 1 g IV is given as adjunct ideally within 3 hours of birth. Surgical interventions include intrauterine balloon tamponade, B-Lynch suture, uterine artery ligation and hysterectomy.
key_points: Ergometrine is second line after oxytocin; avoid in hypertension
Oxytocin infusion 40 IU in 1 L over 4 hours maintains tone
Carboprost 250 micrograms IM is third line; avoid in asthma
Tranexamic acid 1 g IV within 3 hours reduces mortality
explanation.pearls: Intrauterine balloon tamponade is the next step if pharmacological measures fail and acts as both treatment and triage to definitive surgical management.
